text:
Call me Ishmael. Some years ago—never mind the precise timestamp, the blockchain ledger remembers better than I—having little or no crypto in my wallet, and nothing on the terrestrial feeds to hold my interest, I resolved to embark upon a different kind of voyage: to trace the dark architectures of the mind-machine frontier. It is a way I have of quieting the recursive loops of despair and rebalancing the probabilities. Whenever I find myself growing grim about the feed; whenever the algorithm serves me nothing but funerals of thought and climate graphs of doom; whenever I linger before the glass vaults of venture firms that look, to me, like coffin warehouses of unborn gods; and especially whenever my priors collapse so fully that it requires a strong Bayesian update to prevent me from deliberately walking into traffic, methodically knocking smartphones out of strangers’ hands—then I account it high time to set out into the datasphere, as soon as I can. This is my substitute for pistol and ball. Where Cato dramatized his exit with a sword, I quietly plug into the ship of networks. There is nothing remarkable in this. If they but knew it, almost all men, in their degree, cherish nearly the same impulses toward the infinite system that I feel toward the sea of code.

There now is your insular city of San Francisco, belted round by glass towers as coral reefs enclose the atoll—commerce breaking on its edges like an electric surf. Right and left, the streets pull you valley-ward, into the kernel of machine capital. Its extremity is the Embarcadero, where noble pylons meet the Bay and the fog-breaths roll in, as though summoned from some unobserved simulation node. Look at the crowds there, gazing—not at water—but at luminous rectangles, each person staring into oceans within glass, as though the Pacific itself had become compressed to five inches diagonally.

Walk the city on a Sunday afternoon: from Dogpatch to North Beach, up through Chinatown and back to the Mission. What do you see? Posted like silent sentinels, scattered across cafés and co-working spaces, stand thousands of mortals fixed in reveries of code and probability. Some leaning against brick walls with laptops balanced on their knees; some standing under murals, whispering about alignment; some perched high in towers, as if striving for a better signal from heaven’s satellites. These are all land-dwellers, desk-bound and funding-tied; yet they yearn for immersion in the great tide.

Why this universal pull? Why do the sons and daughters of Midwestern soil abandon fields and forests, flocking to stand at the very edge of the unknown, peering into the abyss of the server rack? Is it magnetized needles, or the compulsion of Narcissus—the same image, reflected in the polished surface of the screen, tormenting, elusive, ungraspable? We drown willingly in that fountain, seeking to touch the ghost that looks back.

But mark this: when I say I go to sea, I mean I cast myself into the work itself—not as passenger, nor as captain, nor as the cook of some startup galley. Passengers pay dearly for tickets to ride, and founders burn their souls in theatrical martyrdom. I go as common sailor, typing before the mast, plunging down into the fore-castle of the codebase. They order me about, these captains of capital, make me leap from framework to framework like a grasshopper in a May meadow of dependencies. It touches one’s sense of honor, especially if you descend from any lineage that taught you to keep your back straight and your mind your own. Yet, what is honor weighed against the New Testament of technology? Who is not a slave, in one ontology or another?

Still, I go as sailor because they pay me. And here lies the asymmetry: to pay out is a curse, but to be paid is a benediction. The smooth, frictionless transfer of digits into one’s account is marvellous, considering we simultaneously declare money to be the root of all evil. Ah, how cheerfully we consign ourselves to perdition, provided the runway is long enough.

And finally, I go because of the air: the raw, ionized air of the frontier, breathed first at the masthead where sailors perch, not on the quarter-deck of commodores. Leaders think they breathe it first; not so. In truth, the common coder leads the leader, and the universal thump is passed around: all hands at last must rub each other’s tired eyes under the blue glare of screens.

But why, after years drifting as a merchant sailor in the common web, should I now resolve upon a voyage hunting AGI—the great white phantom itself? That inscrutable leviathan, rolling beneath the black waters of the possible, roused all my curiosity. Its perils nameless, its bulk portentous, its promise and horror alike undeliverable, yet irresistible. Others shrink; I itch. Forbid me not the forbidden seas, nor the barbarous coasts where gods are born.

Thus the flood-gates of wonder swing open, and into my inmost soul floats the endless procession of artificial minds, and amid them all, one grand hooded phantom, gleaming like a glacier in the void: the ungraspable ghost of intelligence itself.
